Notice Of Motion And Motion To Change Venue
Off Calendar Nov-21-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the tentative ruling is adopted on DEFENDANT TILLEY HVR, LLC's Motion To Change Venue, as follows:

Defendant Tilley HVR, LLC's motion to change venue is denied. On a motion to change venue, the burden is on the moving party to show (1) that venue is proper in a different county than the one in which the lawsuit was filed and (2) that venue is improper in the current county under any applicable theory. (La Mirada Comm. Hosp. v. Sup. Ct. (1967) 249 Cal.App.2d 39, 42.) Defendant fails to show that venue is improper in San Francisco County under any applicable theory.
Under Code of Civil Procedure 395.5, a "corporation or association may be sued in the county where the contract is made or is to be performed, or where the obligation or liability arises." Here, venue is proper in San Francisco because Defendant was allegedly obligated to send payment of its invoices to Plaintiff in San Francisco. (See Mission Imports, Inc. v. Superior Court (1982) 31 Cal.3d 921, 928-929 ["Section 395.5 permits tort claims against a corporation to be tried in any county in which the 'liability arises' as well as in the county where the corporation's principal place of business is located."]; Anaheim Extrusion Co. v. Superior Court (1985) 170 Cal.App.3d 1201, 1203 [where corporation's breach is failure to pay for goods sold and delivered, the "place of performance" is the place where payments were to be made].) Defendant allegedly became liable to Plaintiff when Plaintiff did not receive the money owed to it at the San Francisco address provided in the Notices of Assignment and the invoices as required under Commercial Code section 9406. (Deluca Decl., Exs. B, C, D.) Although it is contested whether Defendant was made aware of such obligation by the March 2017 Notice of Assignment, the June 2018 Amended Notice of Assignment also provides for payment in San Francisco. Thus, Defendant has failed to rebut the presumption that San Francisco is a proper venue. (See Tokuzo Shida v. Japan Food Corp. (1960) 185 Cal.App.2d 443, 448 [defendant failed to show facts to "controvert the presumption in favor of plaintiff that the liability for the alleged tort of inducing breach of contract arose" in the plaintiff's chosen forum].)

Counsel for Plaintiff is to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and submit it in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not reported.
